narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 37, full-time student status, and school decile rankings from Years 1 and 3—and calculated a 91.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's 5.5—strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the average of about 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting consistently low class standing from early on. In contrast, your LSAT score of 37, which is slightly above the average of 36.7, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction somewhat toward passing, though not enough to offset the other issues. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was a bit below the typical 3.21 and your full-time status aligns with most applicants, these played lesser roles here. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students, who generally rank higher in school deciles and show stronger early academic signals for bar success. This rejection doesn't define your potential, but it underscores how much weight the model places on sustained school performance. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, preparing rigorously for a higher LSAT retake, and seeking targeted tutoring or bar prep resources tailored for students with similar starting points. Many applicants in your position have improved their profiles and succeeded on later attempts—persistence and focused improvement can change the trajectory.
